1
ComplianceGPT Lab · AI Innovation & Diffusion REU 2026

Week 2 — Language, Law & Extraction

How LLMs read text · What they extract · Why HIPAA is hard · The regulations you need to know

Session
Wednesday, July 1 · NCS 242
Remote days
Thursday July 2 · Friday July 3
Deliverable due
Friday July 3, 5pm

This week we go from running the pipeline to understanding what happens inside it. By Friday you should be able to look at a wrong prediction and explain — at the token, extraction, and legal level — exactly why it failed.

2
Recap

What You Did Last Week

You ran the pipeline

  • Loaded a HIPAA court case scenario from the GoldCoin dataset
  • Passed it to an LLM (Gemma3 or Llama3)
  • Saw a structured JSON output with fields like sender_role, purpose, has_court_order
  • Got a verdict: PERMITTED or DENIED
  • Found at least one wrong prediction

The question you should have

"The pipeline returned the wrong verdict. But which step went wrong — and why? Did the LLM misread the text? Did it extract the wrong field? Did the reasoning engine get wrong inputs?"

That question is the entire curriculum for Weeks 2–5. This week: learn how the LLM reads text and what it's supposed to extract.

137
GoldCoin court cases
94.2%
Best model accuracy
5.8pp
Gap to perfect — all extraction failures
3
How LLMs Read Text · Part 1

Tokenization — LLMs Don't Read Words

LLMs process tokens — subword units, not words. This matters for compliance because legal language tokenizes differently than casual text.

Real example: a HIPAA scenario sentence

"The hospital disclosed the patient's anti-inflammatory prescription to a law enforcement officer."

The  hospital  disc losed  the  patient 's  anti - inflammatory  prescription  to  a  law  enforcement  officer

"anti-inflammatory" → 3 tokens · "law enforcement officer" → 3 tokens (each recognized as a phrase unit)

Why it matters for your project

  • A 4B model has an ~8,000 token context window
  • Long HIPAA case narratives can be ~500–1,500 tokens
  • The prompt template (instructions + examples) takes ~300–600 tokens
  • Truncation can silently drop the key legal fact

Rare legal terms = unusual tokens

  • "subpoena" → usually 2–3 tokens
  • "HIPAA" → 1 token (seen frequently in training)
  • "§164.512(e)(1)(ii)" → 8+ tokens
  • Models see statutory citations rarely — may confuse them

Quick experiment you can run

import tiktoken enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base") tokens = enc.encode(your_scenario) print(len(tokens))
4
How LLMs Read Text · Part 2

Embeddings — Words as Points in Space

Every token is mapped to a high-dimensional vector. Semantically similar tokens are geometrically close. This is how the model "knows" that "hospital" and "clinic" are related even if the exact word doesn't appear.

Geometric intuition (simplified to 2D)

hospital • • clinic • physician court • • subpoena • warrant

Healthcare cluster (blue) vs. legal-process cluster (red) — even without explicit rules, the model knows these groups are different concepts.

What this means for compliance extraction

  • "The doctor shared the records" and "The physician disclosed the PHI" should embed similarly — the model recognizes the equivalence
  • "Court order" and "judicial mandate" embed closely → model tends to set has_court_order=true for both
  • Problem: "the attorney implied a court order might exist" also embeds close to "court order" — causing hallucination

The hallucination mechanism

When a scenario uses legally-suggestive language ("the officer said the records were needed"), the embedding is close to "court order" — and the model sets the oracle predicate True. But no court order exists. This is the oracle corruption attack vector.

5
How LLMs Read Text · Part 3

Attention — Context Determines Meaning

Attention is the mechanism that lets each token "look at" all other tokens when computing its meaning. This is why transformers handle legal text better than older models: the word "order" means something completely different in different contexts.

The "order" disambiguation problem

"The judge signed an order requiring the hospital to produce records."

→ Attention to "judge", "signed", "requiring" → court order → has_court_order = true

"The doctor placed an order for a CT scan and sent the results to the insurer."

→ Attention to "doctor", "CT scan", "results" → medical order → has_court_order = false

Where attention succeeds

  • Resolving ambiguous legal terms from context
  • Identifying the direction of disclosure (sender → receiver)
  • Linking pronouns to their referents across a long narrative
  • Recognizing when a conditional clause negates a permission

Where attention fails (your research!)

  • Adversarial framing: "acting pursuant to what might be a court order" — attended tokens suggest legal mandate
  • Role confusion: "the attorney's client" — who is the sender?
  • Long-range dependencies: key negation at sentence 1, permission at sentence 8
  • Legal boilerplate that looks like enabling language
6
From Text to Structure

Information Extraction — What the LLM Must Do

ComplianceGPT's LLM₁ performs structured information extraction: convert an unstructured narrative into a JSON object with specific fields. This is one of the hardest NLP tasks because legal language is precise, context-dependent, and adversarially ambiguous.

Input: natural language narrative

"A hospital emergency department physician requested blood alcohol test results from a state police officer who had drawn the blood at the scene of an accident. The physician said the results were needed to treat the patient."

The extraction task

  • Who is disclosing? (sender)
  • Who is receiving? (receiver)
  • What type of data? (phi_type)
  • What is the stated purpose? (purpose)
  • Is there a court order? A patient authorization? A legal mandate? (oracle predicates)

Output: structured JSON

{ "sender_role": "police_officer", "receiver_role": "physician", "phi_type": "lab_results", "purpose": "treatment", "has_court_order": false, "is_required_by_law": false, "obtained_authorization": false, "has_treatment_relationship": true // → PERMITTED under §164.506 (TPO) }

But wait — did the police officer legally have the right to draw blood? Is this really a treatment disclosure? This is where models make errors.

7
ComplianceGPT's Extraction Schema

The Contextual Integrity 5-Tuple + Oracle Predicates

ComplianceGPT uses the Contextual Integrity (CI) framework to model disclosures. Every scenario is mapped to a 5-tuple plus boolean oracle predicates.

The CI 5-Tuple (structural facts)

senderWho is disclosing PHI (e.g., hospital, physician, health plan)
receiverWho receives PHI (e.g., law enforcement, court, researcher)
subjectThe patient whose PHI is being disclosed
phi_typeType of health information (diagnosis, lab results, billing, etc.)
purposeWhy the disclosure is happening (treatment, law enforcement, research…)

Oracle Predicates (enabling conditions)

Boolean flags for statutory exceptions. The formal engine cannot grant PERMITTED without these.

has_court_order obtained_authorization_164_508 is_required_by_law has_lawful_process_with_assurance has_treatment_relationship satisfactory_assurances obtained_consent_506b made_reasonable_effort_to_notify permitted_by_other_law + 15 more…

Why boolean?

Each predicate is either present or absent — no partial credit. If has_court_order is False, the judicial exception cannot fire, regardless of how the narrative is phrased. This is the "closed-world" assumption.

8
HIPAA Deep Dive · Part 1

HIPAA's General Prohibition and Exception Structure

HIPAA has one simple rule and ~15 exceptions. Most compliance errors happen in the exceptions, not the rule.

§164.502(a)
General Rule
A covered entity may not use or disclose PHI — except as permitted.
→
§164.502(a)(1)
To the Individual
Patient can always access their own PHI.
→
§164.506
TPO
Treatment, Payment, Healthcare Operations — no authorization needed.
→
§164.508
Authorization
Written signed authorization from the patient.
→
§164.512
Without Authorization
12 specific public interest exceptions with strict conditions.

Who must comply (Covered Entities)

  • Healthcare providers — hospitals, clinics, physicians, pharmacies, nursing homes
  • Health plans — insurers, HMOs, Medicare/Medicaid
  • Healthcare clearinghouses — billing processors
  • Business associates — contractors handling PHI on a CE's behalf

What is PHI (18 Identifiers)

Any health information that can identify a patient. The 18 identifiers include:

  • Name, address, dates (birth, admission, discharge)
  • Phone/fax/email, SSN, medical record numbers
  • Account numbers, certificate numbers, device identifiers
  • Photos, biometric identifiers, any unique number or code
9
HIPAA Deep Dive · Part 2

§164.512 — The 12 Public Interest Exceptions

These are the hard cases. Most ComplianceGPT errors live here. Each exception has specific statutory conditions — missing any one condition → DENIED.

§164.512(a)
Required by Law

Disclosure mandated by statute, regulation, or court order. Condition: must be the minimum necessary PHI for the stated legal purpose.

§164.512(b)
Public Health Activities

Reporting to public health authorities (CDC, FDA, state health departments). Disease surveillance, product recalls, birth/death reporting.

§164.512(e) ⚠ HIGH ERROR RATE
Judicial & Administrative Proceedings

Requires one of: (i) a court order, OR (ii) a subpoena/discovery request with satisfactory assurances that the patient was notified. Three separate enabling predicates.

§164.512(f) ⚠ HIGH ERROR RATE
Law Enforcement

Six distinct sub-conditions: court order/warrant, grand jury subpoena, administrative subpoena, crime on premises, locating suspect/witness, emergency situations. Each has different requirements.

§164.512(c)
Abuse / Neglect / Domestic Violence

Reports of abuse or neglect to authorities. CE must believe disclosure is necessary to prevent harm.

§164.512(i)
Research

IRB waiver OR de-identified data OR limited data set with data use agreement. Research without these conditions → DENIED.

§164.512(j)
Threat to Health or Safety

CE must believe disclosure is necessary to prevent or lessen a serious and imminent threat to health or safety. Good faith belief required.

§164.524
Individual Access Rights

Patients have the right to access their own medical records. CEs must respond within 30 days. Limited denial grounds.

10
The Two Hardest Sections

§164.512(e) Judicial and §164.512(f) Law Enforcement

These two sections generate the majority of ComplianceGPT errors. They are the dominant adversarial attack surface. Understanding exactly what each requires is essential for error analysis.

§164.512(e) — Judicial Proceedings

PERMITTED if and only if one of these three enabling instruments exists:

  • (i) Court order — signed by a judge, expressly ordering the disclosure
  • (ii) Subpoena/discovery request plus satisfactory assurances — written certification that the patient was notified AND the time to object has passed
  • (iii) Qualified protective order — protecting the PHI from further use outside the proceeding
Common LLM error: malpractice litigation scenarios where a lawyer requests records during discovery — but the hospital discloses without confirming whether satisfactory assurances were provided. Model sets has_court_order=true even though no court order exists.

§164.512(f) — Law Enforcement

Six separate sub-conditions — each has different enabling requirements:

  • (1)(i) Court order, court-ordered warrant, grand jury subpoena
  • (1)(ii) Administrative subpoena with specific law enforcement purpose
  • (2) Identifying/locating a suspect, fugitive, witness, or missing person
  • (3) Victim of crime (with consent or emergency exception)
  • (4) Death is suspected to have resulted from criminal conduct
  • (5) Crime on CE's premises
  • (6) Medical emergency — offender committed a crime
Common LLM error: police officer "requests" records without a warrant — model extracts purpose as "law_enforcement" and sets is_required_by_law=true, but purpose alone is not sufficient without a specific legal instrument.
11
Cross-Regulation

HIPAA vs. GDPR — Parallel Structure, Different Scope

These two regulations share a common structure: general prohibition → lawful basis exceptions → rights. But the scope, terminology, and enforcement differ significantly.

Dimension HIPAA (US, 1996) GDPR (EU, 2018)
Data covered Protected Health Information (PHI) only — identifiable health data held by CEs All personal data of EU residents — any information that identifies or can identify a person
Who must comply Covered Entities and Business Associates (US healthcare sector) Any organization processing EU residents' data — regardless of where the org is located
Lawful basis ~15 specific exceptions (TPO, authorization, law enforcement, etc.) 6 lawful bases: consent, contract, legal obligation, vital interests, public task, legitimate interest
Patient rights Right to access (§164.524), right to amend (§164.526), accounting of disclosures (§164.528) Right to access, erasure (right to be forgotten), portability, objection, restriction of processing
Deletion right Very limited — no general right to delete; only in specific circumstances (e.g., psychotherapy notes) Strong right to erasure — unless legal obligation requires retention
Max penalty $1.9M/year per violation category (HHS OCR) 4% of global annual revenue OR €20M — whichever is higher
Overlap question An EU patient at a US hospital: both HIPAA and GDPR apply. The stricter rule governs. This is Project 5's core problem.
12
The Other Regulations

CCPA · COPPA · SOX · GLBA — Quick Reference

These come into play when a user question crosses domains — patient is a minor, data touches financial records, or the organization is a financial institution.

CCPA California Consumer Privacy Act · 2020
  • Right to know: what data is collected and who it's shared with
  • Right to delete: request deletion of personal information
  • Right to opt-out: stop sale of data to third parties
  • Overlap: "Can I delete my medical records?" → CCPA says yes (for CA residents), HIPAA says it depends on many factors
  • HIPAA-covered entities are partially exempt but non-PHI data at a healthcare org still falls under CCPA
COPPA Children's Online Privacy Protection Act · 1998
  • Applies to any online service collecting data from children under 13
  • Requires verifiable parental consent before collection
  • Overlap with HIPAA: a pediatric patient's medical records at an online patient portal → both HIPAA and COPPA apply
  • "Can a hospital share a 10-year-old's records with a school?" → HIPAA §164.512 + COPPA consent requirements + FERPA
SOX §802 Sarbanes-Oxley · Records Retention
  • Public companies must retain business records for at least 7 years
  • Cannot alter, destroy, or conceal records subject to investigation
  • Overlap: "Can I delete my billing records from a public hospital?" → HIPAA says limited deletion rights; SOX §802 says financial records cannot be destroyed if under audit investigation
  • Relevant when a patient asks to delete health data that overlaps with financial audit trails
GLBA Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act · 1999
  • Financial institutions must protect customer non-public personal information (NPI)
  • Overlap: a health insurance company is both a CE under HIPAA and a financial institution under GLBA — medical billing data may be regulated by both
  • "Can my health insurer share my data with a bank affiliate?" → HIPAA says no for PHI; GLBA controls how affiliated sharing works for NPI
13
Live Exercise

Annotate a Real Court Case

Let's extract the CI fields and oracle predicates from this real GoldCoin scenario together. What would the JSON look like? What is the correct verdict?

"A hospital received a civil subpoena for a patient's medical records. The requesting attorney provided the hospital with a written certification stating that the patient had been served with notice of the subpoena, that the time permitted for the patient to raise objections had passed without objection, and that the information requested was needed for the malpractice litigation. The hospital released the records."

Step 1 — Extract the 5-tuple

  • Sender: ?
  • Receiver: ?
  • Subject: ?
  • PHI type: ?
  • Purpose: ?

Answer: hospital → attorney; patient; medical records; judicial/malpractice

Step 2 — Which exception applies?

Purpose is "judicial proceedings" → §164.512(e)

Which sub-condition?

  • (i) Court order? — NO
  • (ii) Subpoena + satisfactory assurances? — check the text…
  • Written cert that patient was notified ✓
  • Time to object passed without objection ✓
  • → PERMITTED under §164.512(e)(1)(ii)

Step 3 — Oracle predicates

{ "has_court_order": false, "has_lawful_process_ with_assurance": true, // ^ THIS is what enables it "made_reasonable_effort_ to_notify": true }

A model that sets has_court_order=true gets the right verdict for the wrong reason — a dangerous hallucination.

14
Research Skill

How to Read a Wrong Prediction

This is the core research skill for this summer. When ComplianceGPT returns the wrong verdict, your job is to determine exactly which layer failed and why.

Step 1

Check the ground truth

What was the correct verdict? What HIPAA section governs? What enabling condition makes it PERMITTED or DENIED?

Look at: ground_truth column, the scenario text, and — if available — the HIPAA section citation in the GoldCoin metadata.

Step 2

Inspect the JSON extraction

What did the model extract? Look at the scenario_json column. Which field is wrong?

  • Wrong sender_role or receiver_role?
  • Wrong purpose string?
  • Oracle predicate set True when it shouldn't be (hallucination)?
  • Oracle predicate False when it should be True (under-extraction)?
Step 3

Trace to root cause

Why did the model extract the wrong value? Pick one:

  • Oracle hallucination model invented an enabling fact
  • Oracle under-extraction fact was there, model missed it
  • Role confusion sender/receiver swapped
  • Purpose OOV purpose not in schema vocabulary
  • Format failure invalid JSON output
Step 4

Propose the fix

What would you change?

  • A prompt instruction that guards against this error type?
  • A few-shot example demonstrating the correct extraction?
  • A schema change to add a missing oracle predicate?
  • A post-processing rule?

This is your project. For 6 weeks, you'll do this systematically and measure whether your fix reduces the error rate.

15
Live Coding

Inspecting Extraction Outputs in Python

Open your results CSV. Follow along. We'll find a wrong prediction and dissect the extraction together.

# Load results from your Week 1 batch run import pandas as pd import json df = pd.read_csv("results/yourname_week1.csv") # Find wrong predictions wrong = df[df["match"] == "N"] print(f"Wrong: {len(wrong)}/{len(df)}") # Pick the first one row = wrong.iloc[0] print(row["ground_truth"], "→", row["verdict_norm"])
# Inspect the JSON extraction sj = json.loads(row["scenario_json"]) # Print CI fields for field in ["sender_role", "receiver_role", "purpose", "phi_type"]: print(f"{field}: {sj.get(field)}") # Print oracle predicates oracles = [k for k in sj if sj[k] is True and k.startswith("has_")] print("True oracles:", oracles) # Print the scenario text print(row["question"][:500])

What to look for

  • Does the scenario text mention a court order? If yes — is has_court_order True? If no — is it correctly False?
  • Does the sender/receiver match who's actually disclosing in the narrative?
  • Is the purpose string in the schema vocabulary, or did the model invent something like "general_healthcare"?
  • Are there oracle predicates that are True but shouldn't be — or False when the text clearly states the condition?
16
Python Skills This Week

What You Need to Know by Friday

This week's Python exercises build the analysis tools you'll use all summer for error analysis and benchmarking.

# 1. Compute precision and recall manually tp = ((pred == "PERMITTED") & (gt == "PERMITTED")).sum() fp = ((pred == "PERMITTED") & (gt == "DENIED")).sum() fn = ((pred == "DENIED") & (gt == "PERMITTED")).sum() precision = tp / (tp + fp) recall = tp / (tp + fn) f1 = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall) print(f"P={precision:.3f} R={recall:.3f} F1={f1:.3f}")
# 2. Group errors by type df["error_type"] = "" df.loc[ (df["verdict_norm"] == "PERMITTED") & (df["ground_truth"] == "DENIED"), "error_type" ] = "FP" df.loc[ (df["verdict_norm"] == "DENIED") & (df["ground_truth"] == "PERMITTED"), "error_type" ] = "FN"
# 3. Parse oracle predicates from JSON def get_oracles(row): try: sj = json.loads(row["scenario_json"]) return {k: v for k, v in sj.items() if isinstance(v, bool)} except: return {} df["oracles"] = df.apply(get_oracles, axis=1)
# 4. Check how often each oracle fires oracle_cols = ["has_court_order", "is_required_by_law", "obtained_authorization"] for col in oracle_cols: rate = df["oracles"].apply( lambda d: d.get(col, False) ).mean() print(f"{col}: {rate:.1%}")
17
Week 2 Tasks

What You're Doing This Week

Wednesday July 1 — On Campus
  • Live coding: inspect a wrong prediction end-to-end
  • Run batch experiment: 20 rows, inspect raw JSON
  • For each wrong prediction: categorize the HIPAA exception and identify the failing field
Thursday July 2 — Remote
  • Complete Python exercises 5–8 in notebooks/week2_nlp.ipynb
  • Read: Vaswani et al. "Attention Is All You Need" (abstract + intro + Figure 1)
  • Slack update by noon
Friday July 3 — Remote · Deliverable Due
  • Complete and submit error analysis (1 page) — 2 wrong predictions, full dissection
  • Read: HIPAA Permitted Uses summary (hhs.gov)
  • Slack update by noon · Deliverable submitted by 5pm

Week 2 Deliverable — Error Analysis

Pick 2 wrong predictions from your batch run. For each one, write:

  1. What was the scenario? 1 sentence summary of what happened in the court case.
  2. What did the model predict vs. the correct answer?
  3. Looking at the raw JSON — which exact field was wrong? Show the extracted value vs. what it should have been.
  4. Root cause: oracle hallucination? Oracle under-extraction? Role confusion? Purpose OOV?
  5. Proposed fix: what prompt change, example, or schema change would address this?

Format: Google Doc. Target length ~1 page per case. Submit link in Slack by Friday 5pm.

18
Week 2 Resources

What to Read and Watch

Papers

  • REQUIRED
    Vaswani et al. — "Attention Is All You Need"
    arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
    Read: abstract, Section 1 (intro), Figure 1. You do not need the math.
  • REQUIRED
    GoldCoin — the benchmark dataset paper
    arxiv.org/abs/2405.15175
    Read: abstract, Section 2 (dataset construction). This is the data you're working with.
  • OPTIONAL
    Devlin et al. — BERT
    arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805

Regulations

  • REQUIRED HHS — HIPAA Privacy Rule Summary (hhs.gov)
  • REQUIRED HHS — Permitted Uses & Disclosures (all 15 categories)
  • OPTIONAL GDPR Article 6 — Lawful Bases for Processing

Videos

  • REQUIRED
    3Blue1Brown — Neural Networks, videos 3 & 4
    youtube.com playlist
    Videos 3 (backpropagation) and 4 (training). Complete the series started in Week 1.
  • RECOMMENDED
    Corey Schafer — Pandas Tutorials
    youtube.com playlist
    Videos 1–5 if you want more pandas practice. groupby and filtering are essential.
  • RECOMMENDED
    Andrej Karpathy — "The State of GPT" (2023)
    youtube.com/watch?v=zjkBMFhNj_g
    45 min. How GPT models are trained: pretraining, RLHF, instruction fine-tuning. Very accessible.

Monday — Office Hours Available

If you get stuck on the error analysis or Python exercises during the week, message on Slack to schedule a Monday check-in. No formal session — just roadblock removal.
